segments.s618-g137: پیام بزرگان (literally “message of the great ones”) is the subject/possessive phrase. به خاقان marks the indirect object ‘to/for the Khagan’. بداد is an archaic narrative past-form of ‘دادن’ (to give) placed at the end as the main verb, so the whole segment means “the elders' message was given to the Khagan.”
segments.s618-g138: دل شاه ترکان is a genitive chain “the heart of the king of the Turks.” بدان is an adverbial/compound (literally ‘to that’ / ‘thereby, because of that’) linking this clause to the previous one. گشت is the past of شدن ‘became’ and شاد is the predicative adjective ‘happy/glad’ — together: “thereby the Turkish king’s heart became glad.”
segments.s618-g139: وزان is a connective adverb ‘and/from that’ linking to what came before. جستن here is an infinitive/participial form ‘seeking/pursuing’ used adjectivally, modified by تیز ‘swift’, and both modify the name بهرامشاه — ‘Bahram-shah, swift in pursuit’ (word order is inverted for poetic effect).
segments.s618-g140: گریزان is a participle ‘fleeing’ describing تازیان (the Arabs). بشد is the narrative past/auxiliary ‘it became/it happened’ used to mark the event (‘they fled’). با سپاه is an instrumental/adverbial phrase ‘with an army/host’ indicating accompaniment.
segments.s618-g141: به پیش is a prepositional phrase ‘before / in the presence of’. گرانمایه is an adjective ‘esteemed/precious’ modifying خاقان. بگفت is the past narrative verb ‘he said/spoke’ (classical narrative form), so the segment means “he spoke before the revered Khagan.”
segments.s618-g142: دل و جان خاقان is a coordinated possessed phrase ‘the heart and life (soul) of the Khagan’. چو is a simile ‘like/as’, گل is the simile noun ‘flower’, and برشکفت is the past verb ‘burst open / blossomed’ used metaphorically for joyful emotion — ‘the Khagan’s heart and soul blossomed like a flower.’
segments.s618-g143: به ترکان is the indirect object ‘to the Turks’. چنین is an adverb ‘thus/so’. گفت is the reporting verb ‘said’, and خاقان چین is the subject phrase ‘the Khagan of China’ placed after the verb (a common poetic inversion). The whole line reads “To the Turks thus said the Khagan of China.”
segments.s618-g144: که introduces reported content ‘that’. ما is the first-person plural subject ‘we’. برنهادیم is the first-person plural past ‘we placed/raised upon’ (برنهاد + -یم). بر چرخ زین is a prepositional phrase with بر ‘on/upon’, چرخ ‘the sky/heaven/wheel’ and زین ‘saddle’ (object placed) — literal: ‘we placed a saddle upon the sky/wheel.’
segments.s618-g17: This is a temporal clause introduced by چو (= "when"). Word order is prepositional locative + temporal conjunction + subject + verb + source: به ایران (to/into Iran) sets the locus, چو آگاهی آمد (when information/news came) is the core clause, and ز روم (from Rome) names the source. Note the poetic preposition ز (instead of modern از).
segments.s618-g18: A coordinated list of sources using repeated poetic ز (= from) for emphasis: ز هند و ز چین و ز آباد بوم. آباد بوم is an adjectival phrase meaning "prosperous/inhabited land" (آباد modifies بوم). The conjunction و links the items; the phrase continues the source list begun in the previous segment.
segments.s618-g19: The conjunction که (= "that/which/so that") introduces a reported clause describing Caesar's action. قیصر is the subject; سپه کرد is an idiomatic past construction meaning "raised/mustered an army" (literally "made an army"); و links the second verb phrase لشکر کشید, where کشید is the past of کشیدن and here idiomatically means "to draw/march (an army)". Both verbs share the same subject قیصر.
segments.s618-g20: Another source phrase followed by the result/appearance: ز چین و ختن (from China and Khotan) is a prepositional coordination; لشکر آمد پدید is a two‑verb predicate where لشکر (an army) is the grammatical subject and آمد پدید (lit. "came, appeared") expresses emergence — a common poetic pairing (آمد + پدید) meaning "appeared/arrived".
segments.s618-g21: This is a relative clause begun by هرانکس که (= "whoever/whosoever, who"). به ایران gives the domain ('in Iran'). بد پیش‌رو is an epithet: پیش‌رو means "foremost/one who goes ahead," and بد is an attributive adjective giving a negative or grave coloring (here a poetic characterization). The whole segment names which people are meant: "whoever was foremost."
segments.s618-g22: A coordinated apposition specifying kinds of people: ز پیران و از نامداران نو = "from the elders and from the newly‑renowned." Note the slight variation of prepositions (ز … و از …) which is common in poetic diction; نو ("new") qualifies نامداران ("the famous/renowned ones").
segments.s618-g23: Subject + prepositional phrase + verb: همه (all/everyone) is the plural subject, پیش (here "in the presence of/before") governs the addressee, and بهرام گور is the two‑word proper name (بهرام + epithet گور). آمدند is the past plural verb agreeing with همه.
segments.s618-g24: An adverbial phrase describing manner: پر از ("full of") takes a coordinated list of abstract nouns (خشم, پیکار, شور). The final آمدند repeats the verb for rhetorical emphasis — together the phrase means "they came full of anger, combativeness, and excitement/tumult."
segments.s618-g169: سپاهش = سپاه + ـش, the possessed noun 'his army' (third-person possessive suffix). همه باره is an adverbial phrase (همه + باره) meaning 'entirely / at every time' or 'altogether', and کرده یله is a compound where کرده is the past participle/past construction ('had made' / 'had put') and یله is an adjective/adverb 'loose, unrestrained' — together 'had set (it) loose' or 'had unleashed'. Word order follows classical Persian: possessor+possessive suffix, then the qualifying adverbial phrase, then the participial predicate plus adjective.
segments.s618-g170: طلایه is 'the vanguard/foremost troops' (subject); نه ... و نه is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor ...'. بردشت here is a noun meaning 'booty/loot, gain' (not a verb), and راحله is the pack-camel / baggage camel — together: 'the vanguard took neither spoil nor baggage.' The verb is left implicit (typical of poetic register) and the negative pair governs the two objects.
segments.s618-g171: A asyndetic list joined by repeated و: 'شکار و می‌و مجلس و بانگ چنگ' names pleasures/activities. می‌و is the archaic/poetic form related to 'fruit' (often used figuratively for wine), مجلس is 'gathering/assembly', and بانگ چنگ is literally 'the cry/sound of the harp' (بانگ + noun). This is a paratactic enumeration typical of epic diction.
segments.s618-g172: شب و روز 'night and day' is an adverbial time-phrase; ایمن نشسته is a verbless / stative expression where نشسته (past participle ‘seated, settled’) functions predicatively with ایمن 'safe' to mean 'sits safely' or 'is safely seated'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' and جنگ is the source ('safe from war'). Order is typical Persian: time phrase, predicate (adjective + participle), prepositional complement.
segments.s618-g173: همی is an adverb meaning 'still / continually' prefacing the clause. باژ ایرانیان چشم‌داشت is a compact nominal phrase: باژ 'cry' (or 'clamor') modifying چشم‌داشت 'expectation' with ایرانیان the agent/possessors ('the Iranians' cries/expectations'). The clause is nominal/elliptical (no explicit verb), common in poetry to convey ongoing action.
segments.s618-g174: ز introduces the cause 'because of'; دیر آمدن is a verbal noun/infinitive phrase 'the late coming/arrival'. دل پر از خشم داشت is a simple past clause: دل 'heart' + پر از 'full of' + خشم 'anger' + داشت (past of داشتن) functioning as the copular/possession verb 'had' (i.e. 'his heart was full of anger'). Classical Persian uses داشت where Modern Persian might use بود.
segments.s618-g9: به بازی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into/for play' and functions as the complement describing how the action is done. همی is the archaic/poetic continuous/habitual marker (present/imperfective). بگذارند is third-person plural present (classical form) 'they put/they set/they toy (with)', with the plural subject omitted. جهان is the direct object (here 'the world') placed after the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s618-g10: نداند is third-person singular present 'does not know' (negative of دانستن); its subject is the previous جهان ('the world'), so the clause resumes that topic. همی again marks ongoing/habitual aspect. آشکار و نهان are coordinated adjectives used substantively: 'the manifest (what is clear) and the hidden.'
segments.s618-g11: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' خاقان چین is a nominal phrase 'the Khagan of China' (چین names the realm), and follows the usual head-noun + modifier pattern in poetry. این سخنها is the object 'these words,' and شنید is the past tense 'he heard' (here with the nuance 'learned/heard of').
segments.s618-g12: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' indicating source. چین و ختن list the regions 'China and Khotan' as the source of forces. لشکری is the object 'an army/troops' (indefinite), and برگزید is a past tense verb 'he chose/raised/selected' — here 'raised/assembled' an army.
segments.s618-g13: درم داد is a simple past clause 'he gave (dirhams/money)' with درم the direct object and داد the past of 'to give.' The conjunction و links the next clause. سر سوی ایران نهاد is a light-verb construction: سر ('head/face') + نهاد (past of نهاد 'placed') with سوی ایران 'toward Iran' — idiomatically 'he turned/set his head toward Iran' = 'he set out for Iran.'
segments.s618-g14: کسی را نیامد is an impersonal idiom meaning 'it did not occur to anyone' or 'no one came to (have) ...' را is the object/topic marker attached to کسی. ز introduces the source/content 'from/of Bahram,' so ز بهرام یاد = 'the memory of Bahram'; نیامد is the negative past 'did not come (to mind).' Overall: 'no one remembered Bahram.'
segments.s618-g15: وزان سوی is a connective phrase 'and/on that side' linking to previous events. قیصر is the subject 'the Caesar/emperor.' سپه برگرفت is a verbal phrase where سپه 'army' is the object and برگرفت (past of برگرفتن) 'took up/raised' — 'the Caesar raised an army on that side.'
segments.s618-g16: همه کشور روم is the subject phrase 'the whole country/realm of Rome.' لشگر گرفت is a compact past clause: لشگر 'an army/troops' as object and گرفت (past of گرفتن) 'took/raised' — idiomatically 'mustered troops' or 'took up arms.'
segments.s618-g73: The phrase begins with the connective prepositional phrase 'وزان' ('and/from that') that links this action to what came before. Word order is archaic/classical Persian: the place phrase 'جایگه' (place) precedes the subject 'لشکر' (army) and the verb 'کشید' (past 3rd sg). 'اندر' is a locative preposition here ('into/among/in'), so the clause means roughly 'from that place the army moved in'. The verb 'کشید' is a simple past 3rd-sg used in an older poetic register.
segments.s618-g74: 'سوی' is the preposition 'toward'; it governs the place name 'آذرآبادگان' (Azarbayjan). The verb 'پرکشید' is an intransitive past 3rd-sg in poetic register meaning 'flew off' or 'sped away', so the clause means 'it rushed toward Azarbayjan'.
segments.s618-g75: 'چو' here introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'when'. The source phrase 'از پارس' ('from Pars') precedes the noun 'لشکر فراوان' ('a large army') which is the object, and the verb 'ببرد' (3rd-sg form of برد with the prefixed 'ب') expresses 'he took/led' — a subjunctive/optative or narrative present/past form used in older Persian to indicate the action of taking an army from Pars.
segments.s618-g76: 'چنین بود' is a fixed phrase 'thus was'; 'بود' is past 3rd-sg 'was'. The noun 'رای' ('opinion/counsel') takes the genitive/possessive 'بزرگان و خرد' ('of the great and of wisdom'), so the whole means 'such was the counsel/opinion of the elders and of wisdom.'
segments.s618-g77: The clause begins with the relative/subordinating particle 'که' ('that/who/for') introducing the reason: 'بهرامشاه' is the subject and 'بگریخت' is the archaic past form meaning 'fled'. 'از جنگ' ('from the battle') is the source phrase. The construction is a simple past narrative: 'who had fled from the battle, Bahram-shah...'.
segments.s618-g78: 'وزان' ('and/from that') links to the prior clause; 'سوی آذر' is a prepositional phrase 'toward Azar' and 'کشیدست' is an archaic perfect/past 3rd-sg form meaning 'took' or 'made (his way)'; 'راه' (path/road) is the direct object. Literally: 'and from there he took the road toward Azar.'
segments.s618-g79: 'چو' here means 'when'; the structure is subject 'بهرام' + object 'رخ' ('face/visage') + prepositional direction 'سوی دریا' ('toward the sea') followed by the past verb 'نهاد' (literally 'placed' — used idiomatically 'to set/turn one's face toward'). This yields 'When Bahram turned his face toward the sea.'
segments.s618-g80: The nominal 'رسولی' functions as an indefinite subject 'a messenger' (poetic form), 'ز قیصر' is the source 'from the Caesar', and 'بیامد' is the past tense 'came'. The simile 'چو باد' ('like the wind') follows to modify the verb, giving 'a messenger from the Caesar came like the wind.'
segments.s618-g1: برین‌گونه is an adverbial phrase ('in this manner / thus') placed up front to modify the whole clause. یک چند is a quantifying circumstantial phrase meaning 'for a little while / for some time' (literally 'one few'), followed by the subject گیتی 'the world'. بخورد is Ferdowsian/poetic verbal use of خورد (sense 'befall, happen') in third person singular: here it means 'befalls / happens to' or 'is met with' (word order: adv — temporal quantifier — subject — verb).
segments.s618-g2: A series of parallel prepositional phrases introduced by repeated به ('to/with/in' used idiomatically to mark what the world experiences): 'به رزم' (in war), 'به بزم' (in banquet/revelry), 'به ننگ' (in shame), 'نبرد' (and strife/battle). The repeated به is a poetic device for emphasis and rhythm; و simply joins the items.
segments.s618-g3: پس is a temporal/consequential adverb 'then'. آگاهی is the subject 'news / information' and آمد is the past tense verb 'came' (word order Subject + Verb here). The following prepositional phrases به هند و به روم indicate the places to which the news arrived; روم (Rûm) here refers to Byzantium/Eastern Rome.
segments.s618-g4: A continuation of the destination list with repeated به: 'to the Turks (ترک) and to China (چین) and to آباد بوم' — آباد بوم is a compound description 'inhabited/prosperous land' (آباد modifying بوم). The repetition of به is a rhythmic/poetic device marking each place separately.
segments.s618-g5: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. بهرام را uses the object/ topicalizing marker را to place Bahram in focus ('as for Bahram'). دل is the internal subject 'the heart' and به بازیست is a poetic contraction of به بازی است ('is given to play/pleasure'); بس ('enough/altogether') intensifies that state. Word order and the copular construction are typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s618-g6: This is an elliptical negative-existential clause: کسی را ز گیتی ندارد به کس. ز is the archaic/poetic form of از ('from/in'). ندارد is the present tense 'does not have/possess' (here meaning 'no one holds a claim on him / no one can claim him'). The phrase as a whole means 'there is no one in the world (ز گیتی) who has any claim on/hold of him (به کس)', with را marking the topical/contrastive 'کسی'.
segments.s618-g7: An elliptical negative statement: طلایه نه 'no vanguard' (نه negates existence; the copula 'is/are' is omitted as is common in Persian verse). و دیده‌بان نیز نه repeats the negation with نیز 'also / even' for emphasis and parallelism: 'and no watchman either.'
segments.s618-g8: Another elliptical negative clause specifying place: به مرز اندرون 'at the inner border/frontier' (به marks location here). پهلوان نیز نه continues the negation—'nor is there a hero/champion there either.' The verb 'is/exists' is again omitted, understood from context.
segments.s618-g121: یکی چاره سازیم is a hortative clause: یکی ('a certain/some') + object چاره ('remedy/plan') comes before the verb سازیم. سازیم is 1st‑person plural present-subjunctive/hortative meaning 'let us make/devise'. تا at the end of the segment begins a purpose clause ('so that...') whose verb is realized in the next segment.
segments.s618-g122: بماند is a present-subjunctive (or jussive) 'may/remain' that completes the purpose clause introduced by تا. ز marks source ('from') and combines with تن ('body') so that ز تن = 'from the body'. نگسلد is negative + present-subjunctive (ن‑ + گسلد) meaning 'may not be separated/broken', and پای ما ('our foot/feet') is the logical theme; word order places the verb before the possessor phrase.
segments.s618-g123: یکی موبدی بود is an existential/past statement: یکی ('a certain') + موبدی ('a mobad/priest') with the past copula بود ('was'). نامش is 'his name' (نام + enclitic -ش) followed by the proper name همای; the copula is understood (نامش همای = 'his name was Homay').
segments.s618-g124: This is a string of epithets in apposition to the name: هنرمند ('skilled, artful'), و ('and') links the adjectives, بادانش is the compound با‑دانش contracted to mean 'learned/knowledgeable', and پاک‌رای is another adjective ('pure of reputation / virtuous'). No copula repeats because these adjectives all modify the named person.
segments.s618-g125: ورا is the accusative clitic/pronoun 'him' placed before the verb for emphasis. برگزیدند is a past plural 'they chose/appointed', and ایرانیان (the subject) follows the verb — a common classical Persian word order (object – verb – subject).
segments.s618-g126: که introduces the subordinate clause expressing purpose/result ('so that' / 'that'); آن چاره را is the definite object (آن = 'that', چاره = 'remedy/plan', را = object marker). تنگ is an adverbial/adjectival 'tightly/narrowly' placed before بندد, which is 3rd‑person singular present-subjunctive/jussive ('may bind/fasten'). میان 'between' closes the clause and is spatial/relational ('between').
segments.s618-g127: نوشتند is past plural 'they wrote'. پس is a discourse adverb 'then/afterwards'. نامه‌ای is an indefinite object 'a letter' (‑ای marks indefiniteness), and بنده‌وار is an adverbial/attributive 'in a servile/humble manner' modifying how the letter was written.
segments.s618-g128: This is a prepositional phrase giving origin and destination: از ایران = 'from Iran' (از = 'from'). به نزدیک آن شهریار = 'to near that king' (به + نزدیک used to indicate 'to the presence of / toward the vicinity of' that شاه/king); آن شهریار is the definite addressee.
segments.s618-g89: که is the clause-introducing particle 'that' (introducing what follows). بر ما places the action 'upon us' (preposition + object). همی is the habitual/continuous marker (classical Persian) meaning 'continually' or 'keeps on'. رنج is the object 'suffering/hardship'. بپراگند is the present/stative verb with the b- prefix (classical formation) meaning 'scatter / spread' (here declarative: 'they spread'). Together: 'that they continually spread suffering upon us.'
segments.s618-g90: چرا = 'why'. هم is an additive particle 'also/then' (emphatic link to what precedes). ز لشکر = 'from the host/army' (poetic preposition ز = from). نه گنج آگند is a negated nominal phrase: گنج 'treasure' with آگند 'filled / present' and the negative sense implied (contextually 'is not present'); literally 'there is no treasure (from the host)'. The line asks rhetorically why the army yields no treasure.
segments.s618-g91: به هرجای = 'to/at whatever place' (به + هرجای = wherever). زر is the direct object 'gold'. برفشاند is the verb 'scatters/throws upon' (classical b- prefix verb). همی again marks continuous or habitual action. Altogether: 'he scatters gold everywhere.'
segments.s618-g92: هم here means 'also' or 'yet' (contrastive). ارج جوانی = 'the honor/value of youth' (ارج 'honor/value' governing the genitive/attributive جوانی). نداند is the negative form 'does not know' (classical present/factual negative). همی at the end adds habitual/emphatic aspect. The clause means 'he too does not know the worth/honor of youth.'
segments.s618-g93: پراگنده شد is a compound of the participial/adjective پراگنده 'scattered' with the auxiliary شد 'became' = 'were scattered / became dispersed'. شهری و لشکری are coordinated nouns/adjectival nouns 'townsfolk (or city) and soldiery/army' serving as the subjects of شد. Together: 'the city-populace and the army were dispersed.'
segments.s618-g94: همی jost is the classical imperfective marker + verb: همی جست = 'was rushing/kept running' or 'sought eagerly'. هرکس is the subject 'each person/everyone'. ره مهتری = 'the way/path to preeminence' (ره 'way', مهتری 'preeminence / primacy'). So the clause means 'everyone rushed to (or sought) the path to preeminence.'
segments.s618-g95: کنون = 'now'. ز + و here form the archaic contraction زو (از او) meaning 'of/from him' (classical clitic). نداریم is the negative present 'we do not have' (نداشتن negated possession). ما is the pronoun 'we' (postverbal for emphasis). آگهی = 'news/information'. The whole phrase: 'Now we have no news of him.'
segments.s618-g96: بما = 'to us'. بازگردد is the present/subjunctive-ish form 'returns / should return' (classical subjunctive used for potential/conditional events). بدی ار بهی is a correlative/conditional phrase meaning 'bad (بدی) or (ار = if/whether) good (بهی)' — classical ordering where ار introduces an alternative/conditional. So together: 'if any bad or good (news) returns to us' (i.e. 'whether bad or good, should it come back to us...').
segments.s618-g97: ازان پس (literally “from that then”) is a temporal phrase ‘after that/thereupon’. چو introduces a temporal clause here meaning ‘when’. گفتارها is the plural subject ‘the speeches/discussions’; شد is the past verb ‘became’; کهن is a post‑verbal predicate adjective ‘old/aged’. The word order is subject – verb – predicate, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s618-g98: برین = بر + این ‘upon this/with regard to this’ (here written as بر + ین). بر نهادند is the past 3rd‑person plural verb ‘they set / they took up’ (نهادند = placed/put). یکسر is an adverb ‘all at once / altogether / unanimously’, and سخن is the object ‘speech/statement’. Together: ‘they took up one unanimous statement about this.’
segments.s618-g99: کز ایران = ازِ آنِ ایران ‘from Iran’ (کز is the literary form ‘from which/from’). یکی مرد = ‘a certain man’ (یکی used here as ‘a certain’). با آفرین modifies the man as an epithet ‘with praise / praiseworthy’ (a poetic description). The whole phrase names the envoy sent from Iran.
segments.s618-g100: فرستند is past 3rd‑person plural ‘they sent’. نزدیک here governs the destination sense ‘to/near’ (they sent him to the presence of). خاقان is the title ‘khagan’ and چین is the place name ‘China’. Word order: verb then adverbial/destination phrase, common in narrative.
segments.s618-g101: که introduces a purpose/result clause here (‘so that / that’). بنشین is a jussive/subjunctive form meaning ‘may he cease / settle’ (in poetry the 3rd person subjunctive can appear without the final -د). ازین = از + این ‘from this’ (i.e. ‘this plundering’). غارت و تاختن lists two nouns: غارت ‘plundering’ and تاختن ‘raiding/charging’ (تاختن is an infinitival/nominalized verb used as noun).
segments.s618-g102: ز هرگونه is ‘from every kind / from every quarter’. باید is the impersonal modal ‘it is necessary / one must’. برانداختن is the infinitive ‘to overthrow / to remove / to drive off’. The clause expresses necessity: ‘they must be driven out in every way.’ Note the literary use of the infinitive after باید.
segments.s618-g103: مگر here functions as ‘unless / except’. بوم ایران is the genitive phrase ‘the land/soil of Iran (homeland)’. بماند is the present/subjunctive form ‘remains’ (used in the protasis after مگر). به جای is an adverbial phrase ‘in place / intact’. The clause sets the negative condition: ‘unless Iran’s homeland remains in place…’.
segments.s618-g104: چو begins a temporal clause ‘when’. از خانه = ‘from the house/home’. آواره شد is the past compound ‘became displaced/wandered’ (آواره used adjectivally/predicatively). کدخدای is the subject ‘the village headman’. Poetic word order places the verb before the subject (آواره شد کدخدای = the headman became homeless).
segments.s618-g161: This is a compact poetic word order: the noun phrase 'فرستاده تازان' (the Taz messenger) is followed by the directional preposition 'به' + place 'ایران' and the past tense verb 'رسید' (3rd sing.). 'به ایران رسید' = 'arrived in/at Iran.' The adjective-like noun 'تازان' modifies 'فرستاده' (i.e., messenger of the Taz/raiders).
segments.s618-g162: The clause 'ز خاقان بگفت آنچ دید و شنید' has a somewhat compressed order: 'ز خاقان بگفت' = 'he told the khagan' (ز = 'to/from' in poetic use here marking the addressee), with 'بگفت' the past 3rd-sing verb 'he said/told.' 'آنچ' ('that which') heads a free relative clause 'آنچ دید و شنید' = 'what he saw and heard.'
segments.s618-g163: Poetic/inverted order: the directional phrase 'به مرو' ('to/into Merv') comes first, then 'اندر آورد' (past tense 'brought in') and then the subject 'خاقان' and object 'سپاه.' 'آورد' is past 3rd-singular of آوردن 'to bring'; 'اندر' intensifies the locative 'in/into.'
segments.s618-g164: 'جهان شد' is a verbless/resultative turn with 'شد' (became) giving the sense 'the world was made/felt so' and is followed by the source/cause phrase 'ز گرد سواران سیاه' (literally 'from/about the round/throng of black horsemen'). 'گرد' here functions in the sense of 'around/throng' and links the cause to the result.
segments.s618-g165: The temporal conjunction 'چو' = 'when' introduces 'آسوده شد' ('became at ease/relieved'). The verbless/compound clause 'سر بخوردن نهاد' uses نهاد (past of نهادن, 'placed/put' used here as a light verb meaning 'began') with the infinitive 'بخوردن' ('to eat') and 'سر' functioning as part of the idiom 'سر به X نهاد' meaning 'set about X / began X.' The overall line = 'when they were at ease, they set about eating.'
segments.s618-g166: This short clause is negative in effect: 'کسی را نیامد ز بهرام یاد' literally 'no one came (to mind) the remembrance of Bahram.' 'کسی' here functions as 'no one' in the negative construction; 'را' is the object/topic marker; 'نیامد' is past negative 'did not come' (idiomatically 'did not occur/was not remembered'); 'ز ... یاد' = 'of/from Bahram (the memory).'
segments.s618-g167: The locative phrase 'به مرو اندرون' = 'inside Merv' precedes 'بانگ چنگ و رباب' where 'بانگ' ('sound/cry') is followed by the instruments 'چنگ و رباب' (harp and rebab) that produce that sound. Poetic word order omits the connective particle (ezafe) and places the source instruments after the noun 'بانگ.'
segments.s618-g168: The clause 'کسی را نبد جای آرام و خواب' states that 'for no one was there a place of rest and sleep.' 'کسی را' (no one) is topical/accusative-marked by 'را'; 'نبد' is the poetic negative/existential 'was not/there was not'; 'جای آرام و خواب' = 'a place of calm and sleep.' Word order places the negated copula before the predicate noun phrase in this poetic register.
segments.s618-g41: همی گفت is a frequentative/habitual marker: همی before a verb + گفت (past form) gives the sense 'kept saying' or 'was saying repeatedly'. هرکس کزین پادشا is a relative phrase: هرکس 'whoever/anyone' + کزین ('from/away from this' — ک + این contracted) + پادشا (an older/poetic form of پادشاه, 'king'). The sequence sets up 'whoever (does X) with respect to this king'.
segments.s618-g42: بپیچد is the classical/pre-modern present/subjunctive form with the prefix بـ (here giving a prospective/habitual sense) meaning 'turns/turns away'. The verb governs دل مردم پارسا: دل 'heart' (object) + مردم 'people' (genitive) + پارسا 'pious' (postpositive adjective). Word order places the verb before its object in this clause.
segments.s618-g43: دل شاه بهرام is a possessive/genitive construction where the possessed noun precedes the possessor: 'the heart of King Bahram' (دل + شاه بهرام). بیدار بود is the predicate: بیدار 'awake/alert' + بود (past copula) = 'was alert.'
segments.s618-g44: ازین آگهی is a prepositional phrase 'from/ because of this news' (از + این written here as ین + آگهی 'news/information'). پر ز تیمار بود is a predicative phrase: پر 'full (of)' + ز (poetic 'of/from') + تیمار 'care/anxiety' + بود 'was' = 'was full of anxiety.' The whole clause explains the cause of his alertness.
segments.s618-g45: همی ساختی is an older frequentative/imperfective construction: همی before ساختی gives 'was/kept making or arranging.' کار لشکر نهان is the object: کار 'work/operation' + لشکر 'army' (genitive relation) + نهان 'hidden/secret' (postpositive), so 'secret preparations of the army.'
segments.s618-g46: ندانست is the past negative form of دانستن (here 'did not know'). The clause orders object before subject: رازش 'his secret' (راز + possessive -ش) appears before کس 'anyone' (here functioning as 'no one' in the negative context) and the locative اندر جهان 'in the world' closes the clause — 'no one in the world knew his secret.'
segments.s618-g47: همه شهر ایران is a collective expression 'the whole city/land of Iran' or 'all the towns of Iran' (همه + شهر + ایران). ز کارش is 'because of his action' (ز = because of/of + کار 'deed' + ش possessive 'his'). به بیم is a predicate phrase 'in fear' (به + بیم), so the whole clause means they were seized with fear because of his deeds.
segments.s618-g48: از اندیشگان دل‌شده به دو نیم: از can mark 'among/of' here, اندیشگان 'the thoughtful/thinkers' (a group), دل‌شده is a compound participial/adjectival form from دل شدن 'to have one's heart affected' meaning 'heart-struck/distraught', and به دو نیم literally 'into two halves' — a poetic image meaning their hearts were split in two by fear or sorrow.
segments.s618-g129: سرنامه is a fronted noun meaning 'the heading/preamble of the letter' (it sets the scene); گفتند is past plural 'they said/wrote' and introduces direct speech; ما بنده‌ایم is a present first‑person plural copula construction (بنده + ایـم) meaning 'we are servants' where بنده is a humble self‑reference. Word order places the scene‑setting noun (سرنامه) before the reporting verb.
segments.s618-g130: به فرمان و رایت is a prepositional phrase ('before/at your command and standard') modifying the state expressed by the verb; سرافگنده‌ایم is first‑person plural present 'we are abased/ashamed' formed from the adjective/participle سرافگنده plus the copula ­ایم. The whole clause gives the affective stance toward the addressee's command and banner.
segments.s618-g131: ز چیزی که باشد به ایران‌زمین is a noun phrase with a relative clause: ز ('from') + چیزی ('anything/whatever') + که باشد (relative/subjunctive 'that may be / that exists') + به ایران‌زمین ('in the land of Iran'). باشد here is subjunctive/optative used to mean 'that there may be / that exists'. The whole phrase means 'whatever there may be in the land of Iran.'
segments.s618-g132: فرستیم is first‑person plural subjunctive/optative used for a future/intentional action ('we will send' / 'let us send'); نزدیک here means 'to the presence of / to' (indicating destination); خاقان چین is the title + place ('the khaghan of China'). Word order places the verb before its destination phrase, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s618-g133: همان نیز is a deixis ('that same, too'); با + a list (هدیه و باژ و ساو) is an instrument/ accompaniment phrase 'with gifts, baž and Sâv' where با links the items. This phrase functions as the complement (what will be sent) and continues the plan stated previously. The nouns are coordinated by و.
segments.s618-g134: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('because/since'); با جنگ ترکان نداریم is a negative present construction meaning 'we do not have war with the Turks' (نداریم = 'we do not have / we are not engaged in'); the clause gives the reason for sending gifts. The final تاو is an emphatic/poetic particle attached at the end of the clause.
segments.s618-g135: بیامد is the simple past 'came'; ز ایران is the source 'from Iran'; خجسته is an adjective 'auspicious/blessed' modifying همای; همای here is a proper name (Homay, the queen/mythical figure) rather than the mythical bird sense. The adjective precedes the proper name in the usual poetic order.
segments.s618-g136: خود is an emphatic/reflexive pronoun here meaning 'herself' (emphasizing that Homay herself came); و coordinates the following noun phrase; نامداران پاکیزه‌رای is a compound noun phrase 'renowned/illustrious men of pure‑minded disposition' where پاکیزه‌رای ('pure‑minded') qualifies نامداران ('renowned ones').
segments.s618-g113: شنیدند is a 3rd-person plural past verb (‘they heard’) placed before its subject (ایرانیان), a common word order in classical/poetic Persian (V–S–O). این سخن is the definite object phrase (‘this speech/these words’) with the demonstrative این preceding the noun.
segments.s618-g114: یکی here means ‘one’ or ‘one of them’ (a single person) introducing the actor of the following action; پاسخ کژ is a noun (پاسخ) followed by its adjectival qualifier (کژ = ‘crooked/perverse’). فگندند is an archaic/past plural form of فکندن (‘they threw/they cast’) used idiomatically for uttering or casting forth words; بن functions as a linking/confirming particle here (classical usage, roughly ‘thereupon’ or ‘thus’) rather than a literal noun.
segments.s618-g115: که introduces a reported subordinate clause (‘that’). بهرام is the subject. ز + ایدر is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning ‘from here’ (ز = from, ایدر = here). سپاهی is the object meaning ‘an army/troop’. ببرد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of بردن used in reported speech to indicate the proposed or intended action (‘should/might take, lead’).
segments.s618-g116: the second که introduces the result/purpose clause (‘so that/for’). ما را is the object marker plus pronoun (‘us’). به غم دل is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘into/unto the sorrow of the heart’ (به + غم + دل). بباید is a classical modal/impersonal form meaning ‘it must / it will be necessary’ (here giving a sense of inevitability), and سپرد (infinitival/past-stem of سپردن) is governed by بباید to mean ‘to consign/hand over’ — together ‘it would consign/it would be necessary to consign.’
segments.s618-g117: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’ (classical); خاقان is the subject (‘the khagan’). بیاید is the 3rd-person singular form often used for future or potential events (‘comes’/‘should come’). به ایران is the locative/directional phrase ‘into Iran’; به جنگ specifies purpose ‘to war’ (i.e. ‘to wage war in Iran’).
segments.s618-g118: نماند is a negative/resultative verb meaning ‘will not remain / will not be left’ and here precedes the locative phrase as is common in elevated diction. برین (بر + ین) = ‘on this’ (بر = on/upon, ین an orthographic/poetic form of این), بوم ما = ‘our land/home,’ and بوی و رنگ (‘scent and color’) is an idiomatic pair meaning ‘any trace/sign’ — together: ‘no scent or color will be left on this land of ours.’
segments.s618-g119: سپاهی و نرسی pairs two referents (‘army and — apparently — men/warriors’); the precise reading of نرسی is dialectal/poetic and the provided lemma senses do not match well, so here it functions as a second nominal element (‘men’/‘fighters’). نماند is the negated verb ‘will not remain,’ and به جای (‘in place’/‘in their positions’) indicates that neither soldiers nor men will be left standing in their posts.
segments.s618-g120: بکوبند is a 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive/optative of کوبیدن (classical form بکوبند) meaning ‘they will strike/crush’ (here violent action). بر + خیره indicates the target/scene ‘upon the astonished/onlookers’ (خیره used adjectivally/substantively ‘the astonished’), and ما را به پای literally ‘us at (their) feet’ = ‘they will trample us underfoot.’ Note the object marker را after ما marking the direct object.
segments.s618-g65: بیاورد is a classical/past third‑person singular of آوردن used in epic Persian ('he brought'). چون here means 'like/as' and introduces a simile; قارن برزمهر is a nominal phrase compared by چون (Qarn (as) Barzmehr). Word order places the verb before the simile phrase, a common poetic flexibility.
segments.s618-g66: دگر = 'another' introduces a second person; دادبرزین is a personal name. آژنگ is used adjectivally before چهر to form an epithet ('clang‑faced' or 'with a clanging/battlelike face'), with چهر = 'face/appearance'. The whole segment names the other man and his epithet.
segments.s618-g67: گزین کرد is the compound verbal phrase from گزین کردن + کرد, here past tense 'he chose'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and ایرانیان is the source group; سی‌هزار is the numeral object 'thirty thousand' placed after the source phrase.
segments.s618-g68: خردمند و شایسته کارزار is a nominal/ adjectival phrase describing the chosen men: 'wise and worthy/fit for battle.' Shāyesteh directly qualifies کارزار (battle) as the domain they are fit for; no extra preposition is required in Persian for this attributive relation.
segments.s618-g69: برادرش ('his brother') carries the possessive suffix ش; را is the definite object marker showing that the brother is the (definite) recipient. داد is the past verb 'gave' and تخت و کلاه are the two direct objects ('throne and crown').
segments.s618-g70: که تا here introduces a purpose clause ('so that'); تا + subjunctive expresses intent. بدارد is a present/subjunctive third‑person form of داشتن used for purpose ('may hold/keep'), and نگاه ('gaze/watch') with بدارد yields 'keep watch over' گنج و لشکر = 'treasure and army' are the objects of that watching.
segments.s618-g71: خردمند is an epithet 'wise' placed before the name نرسی (here a proper name). آزاد چهر is a compound epithet 'noble/free of face' (آزاد qualifying چهر); in epic Persian such epithets commonly follow or attach to names to describe appearance.
segments.s618-g72: همش = 'all of him' (his whole being); فر is the technical epic term 'farr' meaning royal glory/radiance, و دین = 'and religion/faith'. بود is the past copula 'was'. هم ... داد و مهر: the second clause adds 'also justice and affection' (داد here is the noun 'justice', مهر = 'love/affection'), so the line enumerates the virtues comprising his character.
segments.s618-g57: دگر here means “another” (marking a new item in a list). مهرپیروز is a proper name (Mehrpīruz). به + زاد is an archaic/genitival expression meaning “born of / of the origin of” (زد/زاد = birth/origin), and را is the definite/topic marker attached to the entire nominal phrase. Word order is topic/marker (دگر) + name + parentage phrase + را.
segments.s618-g58: سوم is the ordinal “the third” placed before the following name. مهربرزین is a proper name (Mehrbarzin). خراد is an epithet 'wise'/'of wisdom' qualifying the name, and را again is the definite/topic marker. The phrase is an ordinal + name + epithet construction.
segments.s618-g59: چو functions as a temporal conjunction “when.” بهرام پیروز is the name with an epithet (Bahram the Victorious). بهرامیان is the plural/collective referring to his followers or the Bahramids; it follows the name as an appositive/collective specification.
segments.s618-g60: خزروان is a plural noun “kings/royalty.” رهام (Roham) is inserted as one of those leaders. با is the preposition “with.” اندیان here represents the archaic/copulative form (from اند, ‘are/they are’) marking presence/existence — effectively ‘they were’/‘were with’. The clause therefore states that the kings, Roham among them, were with (the force).
segments.s618-g61: یکی before each following noun means “one” or “a certain” (introducing individuals in a list). شاه گیلان / شاه ری are nominal phrases “the king of Gilan” and “the king of Rey”; the copula is omitted (a common classical Persian listing construction: ‘one [was] X, one [was] Y’).
segments.s618-g62: که introduces a relative clause “who/that.” بودند is the past plural of 'to be' (“they were”). در رای is “in the counsel/opinion,” هشیار is an adjective meaning ‘prudent/sane/alert’ qualifying that counsel, and پی (here) means “following” (i.e. they were following that prudent counsel). The clause modifies the preceding kings.
segments.s618-g63: دگر again marks “another” item in the list. داد here functions as a name/element of a name (Dād) or a titular noun attached to the personal name برزین (Barzin). رزم‌آزمای is an epithet ‘battle-tested/war-tried’ placed after the name. The whole is a nominal identification: “another (was) Dād Barzin, the battle-tested.”
segments.s618-g64: کجا is a locative/relativizing adverb “whence / from where.” زاولستان (Zābulistān) is a place name (region). بدو here is the clitic-like prepositional pronoun “to him / unto him” (not an imperative). بد (here) means “ill/poorly” or “in bad condition,” and به پای literally “on foot” (به + پای = by foot). Together the phrase says that from Zābulistān he came to him on foot and in poor condition.
segments.s618-g49: همه is the subject 'everyone'. گشته is the participle/auxiliary meaning 'has become' or 'became' and here forms a change-of-state with the following adjective. نومید is the predicate adjective 'hopeless, despairing'. زان is a poetic contraction of از آن 'because of that / from that', and شهریار ('king') is the source that causes the despair — word order is subject + verb + adjective + causal phrase.
segments.s618-g50: This line uses the idiom «خوار گرفتن» (to hold as low/despise). The patient/objects 'تن و کدخدایی' (literally 'person/body and kadhkhodayi' — here 'person/people' and 'the headmanship/local chiefs') precede the verb گرفتند 'they took/held', and خوار ('despised/low') is the predicative adjective showing the result. The subject is implicit (the same 'همه'); the omission of را is common in verse.
segments.s618-g51: پس is an adverb 'then'. آگاهی 'information/news' is the grammatical subject in this impersonal construction. آمد 'came' is the verb and به + بهرامشاه 'to Bahram-shah' marks the person who received the news. The whole phrase means 'then news came to Bahramshah / news reached Bahramshah.'
segments.s618-g52: که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that ...'. آمد is the past 'came'. ز (poetic form of از) + چین = 'from China' and اندر ایران = 'into Iran' (اندر = in/into). سپاه 'army' is the subject of the subordinate clause; word order is typical of poetry: 'that came from China into Iran an army'.
segments.s618-g53: جهاندار 'the world-ruler/sovereign' is the subject. گستهم here is a proper name (Gostaham) and takes the object marker را. پیش خواند is the verbal phrase 'called/summoned forward' (پیش = before/forward, خواند = called). Note: گستهم in the supplied senses list is ambiguous; in this line it is a name, not the verb 'I wandered'.
segments.s618-g54: ز خاقان چین 'from the Khagan of China' names the source; چند is the interrogative 'how many'. با او 'with him' means 'accompanying him/under his command'. براند is the verb 'he drives/led/sends' (here used in the sense 'brings/sends [troops]'); the clause is an indirect question introduced by که, asking how many troops the Chinese khagan has with him.
segments.s618-g55: کجا here = 'where' (in places where). پهلوان بود و دستور بود is a parallel construction: 'there was a hero (champion) and there was order/command' — بود repeated for poetic balance. The clause sets up an expectation of competent leadership.
segments.s618-g56: چو = 'when'. رزم آمدی (آمدی is the past form 'came') = 'when battle came'. پیش rending 'in front / at the forefront' and رنجور 'the wounded/weak/afflicted' is the predicative noun; بود is the copula 'was'. The sense: at the arrival of battle, it was the weak/distressed who were in front — an ironic complaint about absent champions.
segments.s618-g105: چنین گفت نرسی که این روی نیست is structured as a reporting clause plus a subordinate clause. چنين (thus) + گفت (said) precede the speaker's name نرسی (Narsi) in a slightly poetic word order; که introduces the reported content. این روی نیست is a simple nominal clause with این (this/such) modifying روی (face/appearance) and نیست (is not) negating existence — literally, 'such a face does not exist.'
segments.s618-g106: مر این آب را در جهان جوی نیست is an existential statement where مر is a dative/ethical 'to me' (for me), followed by the definite object phrase این آب را ('this water' with را marking definiteness/emphasis). در جهان (in the world) gives the locus, and جوی نیست (there is no stream) is the predicate expressing non‑existence — literally, 'for me, for this water there is no stream in the world.'
segments.s618-g107: سلیحست و گنجست و مردان مرد lists three predicates joined by و: سلیحست and گنجست are adjectival/predicative forms meaning 'there is/it is weaponry' and 'there is treasure' (the -ست ending is an older/polite copular form). مردان مرد is a doubled noun phrase 'men of men' (an intensifying compound meaning 'true heroes' or 'the best of men').
segments.s618-g108: کز آتش به خنجر برآرند گرد is a relative clause introduced by کز ('which/that/from which') modifying 'مردان مرد'. The clause says these men 'برآرند' (they bring forth/draw out) 'خنجر' (a dagger) 'از آتش' (from the fire); به here is instrumental/associative ('with/by'), and گرد is an adverbial/poetic particle giving a circumstantial sense. The verb برآرند is 3rd‑person plural.
segments.s618-g109: چو نومیدی آمد ز بهرامشاه uses چو as a temporal conjunction 'when'. نومیدی آمد is 'despair came' (آمد = came), and ز before the name بهرامشاه marks the source/affected party: 'despair came upon Bahramshah' (i.e., Bahramshah became despairing).
segments.s618-g110: کجا رفت با خوارمایه سپاه is an interrogative clause: کجا رفت 'where did (he/they) go?'. با introduces the accompanying element; خوارمایه (despicable/worthless) modifies or stands for the lowly portion of the troops, and سپاه is 'the army'. The prepositional phrase follows the verb, giving the circumstances of the movement.
segments.s618-g111: گر اندیشه بد کنی بد رسد is a protasis + apodosis (conditional). گر = 'if'; اندیشه بد کنی is a subordinated clause with کنی (2nd person singular subjunctive/present) 'you do/have' and اندیشه بد 'evil thought(s)'. بد رسد uses رسد (3rd‑person singular) meaning 'will happen/befall' with بد 'evil' as the predicate: 'evil will come.'
segments.s618-g112: چه باید به شاهان چنین گشت بد is a rhetorical question. چه باید = 'what ought (to be done)' or 'what is to be expected'; به شاهان = 'for the kings'; چنین گشت بد = 'becomes thus bad' (گشت = 'become', بد = 'bad'). Overall it asks what will become of kings if circumstances thus turn bad.
segments.s618-g153: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a time clause. The order is subject (من) + circumstantial phrase (با سپاه) + verb (اندرآیم) + goal (به مرو): 'when I, with the army, enter to Merv.' اندرآیم is a 1st person present/subjunctive-like classical form 'I enter / I shall enter'.
segments.s618-g154: کنم is the 1st person singular auxiliary/main verb 'I will do / I shall (make/turn)'; here it combines with روی (face/turn) to mean 'I will turn (my face)'. کشو‌ر is the direct object 'country/land'. چو introduces a subordinate comparison/time 'as/when', and پر تذرو is a compact descriptive phrase: پر 'full (of)' + تذرو 'scatter/disperse' (poetic verb) describing the country's state. Overall: 'I will turn to the land when it is full and scatters.'
segments.s618-g155: This is a series of prepositional phrases introduced by به used adverbially ('by' or 'with') listing means/qualities: رای (counsel/plan), داد (justice), رنگ (appearance/complexion), بوی (scent). The repeated به is emphatic/poetic; word order is parallel and nominal.
segments.s618-g156: ابا here is an archaic/poetic verb meaning roughly 'to be reluctant / to refuse' used idiomatically (context implies 'not reluctant' or 'I will not refuse'). آب شیر is a compound 'water and milk' (شیر = milk), اندر آرم is a 1st person present 'I put / I place / I let flow' (classical phrasing with اندر + verb), and به جوی indicates the goal 'into the stream'. Together: 'I will (not be reluctant and) put water and milk into the stream.'
segments.s618-g157: بباشیم is a 1st person plural subjunctive/optative form meaning 'let us be / let us stay'; تا introduces the temporal clause 'until'; باژ 'cry/news/signal' is the object; ایران رسد is a subordinate clause 'reaches/arrives to Iran' with رسد in the 3rd person singular present/subjunctive. The whole segment means 'Let us wait until word reaches Iran.'
segments.s618-g158: همان is the demonstrative 'that very / the same'; هدیه 'tribute/gift' is the noun; و links to the following noun phrase; ساو is a place-name (Saveh) here; شیران in epic diction can mean 'lions' but often 'brave men/warriors' and functions as the subject of رسد 'will arrive'. The clause states that that tribute and the warriors of Sav will arrive.
segments.s618-g159: به مرو آیم is 'I come to/into Merv' with به + place and آیم (1st person present 'I come'). The coordinating و links the following clause زاستر نگذرم where زاستر is a proper name/place and نگذرم is 1st person singular negative-future/ present-subjunctive use of گذشتن 'to pass' or 'to go by' meaning 'I will not pass (by) Zaster / I will not go beyond Zaster.'
segments.s618-g160: نخواهم is 'I will not want / I will not permit' (negative of خواهم); که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'; رنج آید is 'suffering may come' with آید as 3rd person present/subjunctive of آمدن; از لشکرم is a prepositional phrase 'from my army' (لشکرم = 'my army' with ezāfe-like possessive suffix). The clause expresses refusal/wish that no suffering should arise from his troops.
segments.s618-g33: که opens a relative/subordinate clause 'who/that'; دادار گیهان is a nominal phrase 'the Creator of the world' (دادار = Creator, گیهان = world). مرا (written here split as م + را) is the object 'me' (مرا = 'me') attached to the predicate, and یاورست is the predicative phrase 'is a helper' in an older/polite present form (یاور + است → یاورست).
segments.s618-g34: که again introduces a relative clause; the comparative construction از ... برترست means 'is superior to ...'. دانش here names the domain 'knowledge', and برتران refers to 'those superior/most eminent' (i.e. the most learned); برترست is the copula/predicate 'is superior'.
segments.s618-g35: به نیروی introduces an instrumental phrase 'by the power/strength of ...' (به + noun used as 'by/with'). آن پادشاه بزرگ is a demonstrative phrase 'that great king' (آن = that, followed by noun + adjective).
segments.s618-g36: که introduces the content clause; ایران is the theme 'Iran/the realm' placed before the verb (common word order). نگه دارم is a compound verbal expression 'I keep/guard' (نگه = keeping/watch; دارم = 'I have' used with نگه to form 'I keep'); از چنگ گرگ is a prepositional phrase 'from the wolf's claw' (از = from, چنگ = claw, گرگ = wolf) indicating what Iran is being kept safe from.
segments.s618-g37: A list of instruments introduced by به: 'by/with (به) بخت و سپاه' (fortune and army) and again 'به شمشیر و گنج' (by sword and treasure). Repeating به before the second pair stresses both as means.
segments.s618-g38: ز کشور is a locative/source phrase 'from the country/realm' (ز = poetic form of از). بگردانم is a transitive verb 'I will turn away / I will drive away' with first-person ending -م (classical/fixed form here expressing the speaker's action). این درد و رنج is the object 'this pain and suffering' (این = this, درد = affliction/suffering, رنج = suffering/hardship).
segments.s618-g39: همی کرد is a classical narrative/progressive marker: همی + past verb (کرد) gives 'he was doing / he did repeatedly' (continuous past/narrative). بازی is 'play' or 'sport' (here 'made sport of' or 'toyed with'), and the fused form بدان (ب + دان) functions as 'with that' / 'at that' — so کردن بازی بدان همنشان = 'he played/taunted that peer'.
segments.s618-g40: وزو is a contracted poetic sequence (و ز او = 'and from him'); پر ز + خون is a quantifying phrase 'full of blood' (پر = full (of), ز = of/from, خون = blood). دیده سرکشان means 'the eyes of the proud/haughty ones' or 'his haughty eyes' (دیده = eye; سرکشان indicates the proud/rebellious/haughty), so the clause says his (or the proud one's) eyes were filled with blood. This is compact, poetic phrasing rather than literal ablution or similar.
segments.s618-g145: که at the start is an interrogative 'who'. آورَد is a simple past (3rd person singular) 'brought/seized'. بی‌جنگ is a prepositive adverbial phrase 'without war' modifying the action. ایران is the direct object, and به + چنگ ('into the grasp') expresses the result/location of seizure; the clause is a rhetorical question.
segments.s618-g146: مگر here functions as a rhetorical question-particle 'is it that / could it be that'; the finite verb is omitted and understood from the previous clause. ما is the agent 'we'. The repeated به before nouns works adverbially as 'by/with' (means): به رای 'by counsel', به هوش 'by intelligence', and درنگ 'delay/patience' completes the list (the final item lacks a prefixed به for poetic economy).
segments.s618-g147: فرستاده را uses the object marker را after 'messenger' to mark the (definite) recipient; چیز بسیار 'many things' is the direct object phrase. داد is past tense 'gave' (3rd person singular). Word order places the recipient phrase before the object for emphasis.
segments.s618-g148: This is an enumerative continuation: درم داد 'he gave dirhams', then چینی و دینار داد 'porcelain and dinars he gave' — the verb داد is repeated for each item, a common poetic device. چینی here means porcelain/china (a gift), and درم/dinar are named coins/gifts.
segments.s618-g149: یکی here is an indefinite 'someone' / 'a certain person'. پاسخ‌نامه is 'a reply/response letter' and بنوشت is the past 'wrote' (poetic/archaic past form of نوشتن). و گفت 'and said' links to the reported utterance. (Note: the supplied sense entries for بنوشت and گفت do not include their verbal senses, so a sense index is not available for those items.)
segments.s618-g150: که introduces the content of what was said ('that' / in this context functioning like an optative report). با + جان پاکان is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the souls (or lives) of the pure', خرد is the subject/thing hoped for 'wisdom', باد is the optative particle 'may' (a subjunctive/wish marker), and جفت is predicate 'a mate/companion' — together: 'May wisdom be paired with the pure-hearted.'
segments.s618-g151: بدان is the contracted/poetic form of به آن 'to that' (literally 'to that matter/point'). بازگشتیم is the past tense, first-person plural 'we returned'. همداستان is an adjectival/adverbial predicate 'of one mind / in agreement', describing the manner/state of the return.
segments.s618-g152: که introduces the subordinate clause reporting what was agreed. گفت here functions as 'it was said' / 'one said' (past), introducing the content; این فرستاده راستان is the content predicate: 'this messenger is/was truthful' (راستان as an adjective 'truthful/upright'). The clause reports that they judged the envoy to be trustworthy.
segments.s618-g25: بگفتند is a past plural verb 'they said' placed sentence-initial (a common poetic inversion). با شاه is an instrumental/agent phrase 'with the king' marking whom they addressed. چندی درشت is an adverbial phrase: چندی 'for a while' + درشت used adverbially 'roughly/harshly', modifying how they spoke.
segments.s618-g26: که introduces a reported subordinate clause ('that...'). بخت is the subject 'fortune/luck', modified by فروزانت (fروزان + -ت in the text) meaning 'radiant/bright'. بنمود is a past tense verb 'showed/manifested', and پشت ('back') combines idiomatically with بنمود to mean 'turned its back' — i.e. fortune has abandoned (the person addressed).
segments.s618-g27: سر رزمجویان is a genitive construction 'the head/chief of the warriors' (سر = chief/leader). به رزم اندرست is a predicate with به + noun + اندرست ('is in/engaged in battle'), so the phrase says the warriors' leaders are occupied with war. Word order is inverted for poetic effect: predicate follows the possessor phrase.
segments.s618-g28: ترا is written here as two pieces (ت + را) and functions as a dative/possessive 'to you'/'your'. دل is the subject 'heart', and به بازی و بزم اندرست is the predicate 'is given to/occupied with play and banquet' (به marks the sphere or object of inclination). The whole contrasts the warriors' martial concern with the addressee's taste for games and feasts.
segments.s618-g29: به چشم تو is a locative phrase 'in your sight/in your eyes'. خوارست is a predicate meaning 'is low/insignificant' (here functioning as 'is contemptible/worthless'); گنج و سپاه are the logical subjects ('treasure and army'). Note the poetic inversion: the locative + predicate come before the subject.
segments.s618-g30: هم‌ان is a demonstrative/intensive 'that very' / 'the same', introducing the items apposed to what was just called worthless. تاج ایران is a genitive phrase 'the crown of Iran'. هم before تخت repeats 'also' to add 'and likewise the throne (تخت) and گاه (here an abstract 'seat/place' in classical use). The verb is omitted by ellipsis; these nouns complete the contrast begun in the previous clause.
segments.s618-g31: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. داد is the past tense verb 'gave', and پاسخ is the object 'answer/reply'. جهاندار شاه ('world-holder/sovereign king') is a title in apposition to the subject; word order here places the verb and its object before the subject, a common classical/poetic inversion.
segments.s618-g32: بدان is a dative demonstrative 'to those' (here marking the indirect object). موبدان is the indirect object 'the mobeds' (Zoroastrian priests). نماینده راه is a noun phrase 'representatives/agents of the way' in apposition qualifying the mobeds. Together the phrase means 'he gave that answer to those mobeds, who are representatives of the way.'
segments.s618-g81: به + noun phrase + (proper name) indicates motion toward a location: به 'to/into', کاخیش is 'کاخ' (palace) with a possessive suffix (-ش = 'his/of him'), and نرسی here is the proper name Nersi identifying whose palace it is. فرود آوردید is a compound verbal imperative (آورید, 2nd pl. imperative of آوردن 'bring') with فرود (lit. 'descent, alighting') forming 'set down / bring down'. Word order is somewhat compressed/poetic: the destination phrase precedes the command verb.
segments.s618-g82: گرانمایه جایی is an adjectival phrase: گرانمایه 'precious/valuable' modifies جایی 'place, a seat'. چنانچون is a comparative/linking particle 'just as / as', and سزید (past form of سزیدن used predicatively) means 'was fitting / deserved'. Together the clause means 'place it in a precious seat, as befitted him.'
segments.s618-g83: نشستند is past-tense 3rd-plural 'they sat / assembled' (note: the provided lemma list did not include this sense). با introduces accompaniment 'with', and رای‌زن بخردان is a compound grouping meaning 'counsel-givers and the wise' (رای‌زن 'counselor, advisor'; بخردان = 'the wise ones'). The whole segment describes people assembling with advisors and sages.
segments.s618-g84: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'near / close to'; نرسی here is the proper name Nersi (not a verbal form) and functions as the locus toward which people draw near. همه موبدان 'all the mobeds' is the subject/agent that are being near him. Word order is flexible/poetic: the phrase reads 'near Nersi all the mobeds (were / came)'.
segments.s618-g85: سراسر is an adverbial/adjectival 'throughout / all over', modifying سخنشان 'their speech / their words' (سخن + possessive -شان). بد از شهریار literally 'bad of the king' means 'speaking ill of the king' (از marks the target/subject of the ill-speaking). The phrase thus reports that their conversation was entirely unfavorable toward the king.
segments.s618-g86: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause. داد is past 3sg 'gave' (here part of the idiom داد ... به باد دادن = 'to give to the wind' = 'to squander/waste'); او is the subject 'he'. به باد 'to the wind' is a prepositional idiom phrase and آن همه روزگار 'all those days / that whole time' is the thing given/wasted. The clause therefore means 'that he gave (it) to the wind all that time' = 'that he wasted that whole period.'
segments.s618-g87: سوی is a directional preposition 'toward(s)'. موبدان موبد is a poetic/title phrase: موبدان (plural 'the mobeds/priests') with موبد (singular, here functioning as a title 'the chief priest' or appositive) — together indicating the priestly assembly and their head. آمد is past 'came' and سپاه 'the army' is the subject; word order is inverted for poetic effect (directional phrase + addressees, then verb, then subject).
segments.s618-g88: به آگاه بودن is an infinitival/nominal phrase 'to be informed / to make aware' (به + آگاه + بودن). ز marks the source or topic 'about / concerning', and بهرامشاه is the name 'Bahramshah' (the topic of the informing). The whole segment explains the purpose of the army's coming: to inform the priests about Bahramshah / to notify them concerning Bahramshah.
